objects.text: You can reach out and copy up to 3 semblances from people within a few dozen meters of you, but never faithfully. Each copy comes out warped into something related but distinct: someone's raw super-speed might become your own ability to quicken time around yourself rather than your body, someone's fire manipulation might become a knack for burning away your own wounds, and so on. You don't get to choose the exact distortion, and a copied power fades if you stray too far from its owner for too long.

objects.title: Puppeteer's Strings
objects.text: By touching a target, you can attach a nearly invisible strand of aura to them that lets you tug at their limbs and exert partial control over their movement, willing or not. Thicker use requires ongoing concentration and more strands mean more strain; controlling a full body well enough to fight with it is exhausting and unsubtle, but a single misplaced footstep at the right moment can be all you need.

objects.title: Eidolon
objects.text:
Your Semblance grants you access to a seemingly endless arsenal of random Semblances. At any given time, you may possess either 3 stronger Semblances or 5 weaker ones.

While you may choose instantly to replace a Semblance with another, the replacements themselves are always somewhat random, though they naturally tend to suit your current situation and needs.

Each granted Semblance comes with an intuitive understanding of its function, along with subconscious instincts that allow you to wield it with surprising competence from the moment it appears. Although mastery still improves with practice, every power begins at a level comparable to that of an experienced user.

objects.title: Faerie Queen
objects.text:
You can now harvest the Aura of others.

Harvesting a living person's Aura immediately kills them, while harvesting the dead becomes increasingly difficult the longer they have remained deceased.

Once harvested, their Aura becomes permanently bound to yours.

You may then summon Aura Constructs based on those you have harvested. These constructs faithfully replicate the deceased's appearance, combat skill, and complete Semblance, acting with the experience they possessed in life.

You may maintain either three stronger constructs or five weaker ones at a time.

Each successful harvest permanently increases your own Aura reserves by approximately ten percent of the harvested individual's total Aura. This increase is cumulative, allowing your reserves to steadily grow with every soul successfully claimed.

objects.title: How does it work?
objects.text: Your semblance is meant to have all effects be combined to make a cohesive, singular semblance. This is where you outline how it works.
objects.wordPromptText: Please detail how your semblance works

rows.title: Semblance Effects
rows.titleText: This is where you determine the functions of your semblance. Note that semblances are all unique, barring hereditary ones, so you can't truly recreate even a single semblance from canon, only make relatively close copies. Passive effects bypass the usability of the previous section, though active components of primarily passive abilities will not. Semblacne effects will be altered as little as possible to create a singular, cohesive semblance. All effects of a semblance have an Aura cost, though once again, passive and mental effects have a greatly reduced cost. Note that semblances can often take years to master.

objects.title: Smoldering
objects.text: You can instantly light and extinguish small flames within 10m, in addition to creating bursts of heat. You can also control flames within 10m, moving them at a slow speed.

objects.title: Frozen
objects.text: You create thin layers of ice over things within 10m, as well as rapidly pull heat from inanimate objects you touch until they reach freezing temperatures.

objects.title: Windswept
objects.text: You can control air within 10m, letting you concentrate and direct it for weak bursts, currents, and cushions of air strong enough to lift a 10kg (22lb) weight.

objects.title: Shocked
objects.text: For two seconds at a time, you can shift into a state wherein you are made of electricity. In this state, you can't see and can only move in straight lines. For the sake of convenience, this also works on your clothing. This has a cooldown of 10 seconds after each use.

objects.title: Drowned
objects.text: Through your movements, you can control water within 10m, moving it with roughly the speed and force of an average river, but the total mass of the water can't exceed your own weight at any time.

objects.title: Overgrown
objects.text: You can concentrate to selectively make plants within 10m rapidly grow, shaping the growth, such as planting seeds and making them sprout in under a minute, or making vines entangle something. You can also sense nearby plants.

objects.title: Buried
objects.text: Through your movements, you can control rocks within 10m that collectively weight less than your body. Rocks, in this case, meaning solid, mineral-based masses, which does include ice.

objects.title: Weighed Down
objects.text: You can change what direction is "down" for your own body, letting you change momentum and fall in chosen directions. This also applies to things you're wearing or holding, but only while wearing/holding them.

objects.title: Blinded
objects.text: You can create hard light scales over your body which are strong enough to easily deflect small arms fire and can burst into light when destroyed. Compared to Aura, the defensive boost is only mediocre.

objects.title: Ruptured
objects.text: Every few seconds, you can release short-range bursts of heat and sparks from your palms which are roughly half the strength needed to lift yourself off the ground. You are fully immune to your own explosions and only your own.

objects.title: Lapidated
objects.text: You can conjure small rocks and then launch it at a target, with the rock's max speed being roughly 165k mph (102.5 mph). You can do this for three rocks at a time, once every few seconds.

objects.title: Shifter
objects.text: Turning into a bird and back is the classic example, but you can choose any one mundane animal to have the ability to transform into and back.

objects.title: Timeless
objects.text: Your body and mind will not degrade with time, meaning that you will never die of old age. This does not protect you from other forms of death, but does ensure that you will perfectly heal from all wounds given enough time.

objects.title: Bloodline Rebirth
objects.text: After your death, you'll be reborn in your own bloodline. In your new life, you'll retain all memories, Magics, and your semblance, though your physical skills may degrade somewhat before your motor controls return.

objects.title: Healing Hands
objects.text: You can make your hands emit a golden light which heals those you touch, with small cuts healing in seconds and one or two minutes being enough to heal a broken bone. This requires relevant medical knowledge, but it can easily heal yourself and others.

objects.title: Alchemy
objects.text: You can selectively deconstruct and reconstruct the non-living materials you're holding at a molecular level, as long as you understand what they are. For non-solid materials, they must be within a closed container to be transmuted.

objects.title: Genderbender
objects.text: Once daily, you can alter any aspects of your biological sex you may like, from fully switching over from male to female or anything in between. If consent is given, you can do this for another person once daily as well.

objects.title: Trait Change
objects.text: Once daily, via contact, you can change what specific trait a faunus has. As an example, you could swap Blake's ears for a cat tail to help with her balance, or retractable claws, for a biological weapon like her father's. This doesn't do anything for humans. You can use this on yourself if you're a faunus.

objects.title: Dominator
objects.text: Upon defeating a Grimm, you can 'tame' them, removing their impulse to attack others. When in a tamed state, you can force them to assume a more humanoid form which you have rough control over, in addition to Salem no longer being able to command them. This can be applied on up to 10 Grimm at any time.

objects.title: Dark Harvest
objects.text: You can 'harvest' the negative emotions of others, effectively erasing them, building up a metaphorical pool of negativity which you can drain to make a tar-like liquid pool at your feet, from which Grimm emerge if there's enough stored negativity, after which the pool disappears. You would normally need to drain upwards of a dozen people to make even a single Beowulf and can only store enough negativity to make ten of them.

objects.title: Enchantment
objects.text: By sacrificing your ability to use a three-point magic, you can give it to a person to use or an item letting the magic be used by anyone wearing it. If the item is destroyed or the person dies, then the magic will return to you.

objects.title: Runecraft
objects.text: You instinctively know runes that seem to mean nothing. By carving enough of these runes onto an object, you can imbue it with any of several effects based on the other three-point magics you have. With enough months, if not years of trial and error, it's possible to create items comparable in power to the three-point magics. Using the imbued magic will slowly damage the item and weaken the imbued magic, neither of which can be reversed with your other magic options.

objects.title: Condition Restoration
objects.text: Once daily, you can revert an object you're touching to a previous condition you have observed it in. This does not replace anything that's missing but otherwise turns back the clock on the object. The object's mass cannot exceed your own body mass.

objects.title: Silver Eyes
objects.text: Your power seemingly originates from the God of Light and exists to oppose Grimm. When looking at Grimm, you can make a brilliant light flash from your eyes, disrupting the powers of Grimm, turning them to stone, and/or vaporizing them outright. To use this ability, you need to focus on your loved ones and your desire to protect them. This will be passed down to your children by default.

objects.title: The Shadow
objects.text:
In Jungian theory, the Shadow is the repressed, hidden part of the unconscious, containing the parts of yourself you deny or hide from the world. When you're ready, you can physically manifest your Shadow as a doppelganger with a distorted voice and glowing yellow eyes. If you reject it, it will transform into a monster and try to kill you until beaten into submission, after which it reverts back, but is still antagonistic. The shadow's form will be based on a mythological or historical figure from your world and have abilities comparable to an evolved version of your semblance, whether or not you have it yet.

If you truly accept your Shadow as a part of yourself, then you can summon a Persona, the tamed version of the monster your shadow would/did transform into. If you don't have your semblance, your Persona's power is reduced to 25% until you unlock it.

objects.title: More Shadows
objects.text:
Once daily, while touching someone, you can summon another person's shadow, letting them gain a chance to confront and accept the parts of themselves they deny. The rules of how their Shadow and Persona work are identical to your own.

If you develop a strong enough bond with another Persona user whose power you awakened, then your Personas will both become slightly stronger, just enough to notice a difference.

If you don't have an unlocked semblance, then the boost will be reduced to 25%, just like your Persona's base power. Those whose Personas you awaken will also be subject to this reduction if they don't have an unlocked semblance.

objects.title: Home
objects.text:
You have a soulbound key that cannot be lost, destroyed, or stolen, which when inserted into the air as if it were a door, can create a gateway to a pocket dimension with an island floating in an empty, space-like abyss.

Despite the spectacular view of a distant cosmos, there is nothing in that dimension, barring a lone floating island with 10 square kilometers of land. The island inexplicably has a functional atmosphere.

objects.title: Hereditary
objects.text: Much like Ozma and Salem, if you ever have children, they will inherit some of your capacity for magic. You can choose any one to two of your three point-magics for any individual child to inherit. Your descendants will each randomly pass down on one of their magics down to their children.

objects.title: Combo
objects.text: You can combine two or three of the three-point magics obtained here into one, making a power that is more than the sum of its parts.

Your personal gravity is reduced by roughly half. You can jump three times your height, fall from any distance without injury, and walk across fragile surfaces that would collapse under normal weight. The effect is constant; you can't toggle it off.
objects.title: Lightfoot

objects.text: You can speak and understand any spoken language you hear for more than a few seconds, including dead languages and dialects. Written language isn't covered — see Lexicon for that. Useful for negotiations, interrogation, and impressing scholars.
objects.title: Tongues

objects.text: You see through visual illusions, active camouflage, and disguise-based semblances as if they weren't there. Also reveals the true form of shapeshifters. Doesn't penetrate walls, smoke, or regular darkness — just deceptions.
objects.title: Truesight

objects.text: You can become intangible for up to three seconds at a time, passing through solid matter as if it weren't there. You can't breathe while phased, so holding your breath is the practical limit. Cooldown of ten seconds between uses.
objects.title: Phasing

objects.text: You can summon small handheld objects — a coin, a key, a dagger, a folded note — out of thin air. The objects are real and permanent, but you can only summon things you've previously held and 'memorized' (up to ten items). Memorizing a new item forgets an old one.
objects.title: Conjure

objects.text: You can communicate with any non-Grimm animal as if sharing a language. The animal won't necessarily be smart or cooperative, but it will understand you and you it. Works best on mammals and birds; reptiles are slow conversations.
objects.title: Wildspeech

objects.text: By touching someone, you can read the surface thoughts they're actively thinking — what they're about to say, what they're trying to hide, what they're pretending not to notice. Deeper memories and unconscious thoughts aren't accessible. They feel a faint tingle when you do this.
objects.title: Surface

objects.text: While sleeping, you can enter the dreams of someone you've met in the past week. You appear as yourself and can interact with the dream's contents, though the dreamer controls the setting. They'll remember you were there when they wake. Don't try to give them nightmares; they'll remember that too.
objects.title: Dreamwalk

objects.text: You learn the True Name of one person of your choosing (selected at insertion). Speaking their True Name aloud commands their immediate attention and makes any direct magical effect you use on them roughly twice as effective. They also learn yours in exchange, with the same effects. Choose carefully.
objects.title: Truenamed

objects.text: You can teleport to any spot you can see within 25 meters, up to once every few seconds. You arrive with the same momentum you left with, so blinking mid-fall doesn't help. Carried items come with you; living passengers don't.
objects.title: Blink

objects.text: You can alter your apparent appearance — height, face, hair, voice, gender presentation — to anything within human range. The change is purely visual; touch and biology don't change. Maintaining the glamour requires aura. Truesight and similar effects see through it.
objects.title: Glamour

objects.text: Your voice carries a subtle compulsion. Spoken suggestions — 'you don't want to fight,' 'the door was unlocked when you got here,' 'you didn't see anything' — land with unnatural weight, especially on the weak-willed. Strong-willed targets shrug it off and remember you tried. Cannot command direct self-harm.
objects.title: Mesmer

objects.text: You can inscribe a ward on a surface — a door, a wall, the ground — that triggers when crossed by a designated type of creature (Grimm, humans, faunus, etc.). The ward produces a loud alarm and a flash of light, and weakens the crosser's aura slightly. Lasts a week per inscription; you can maintain up to ten at once.
objects.title: Warding

objects.text: You can summon a small magical creature — a cat, an owl, a snake, your choice — that obeys your commands and shares its senses with you when you concentrate. The familiar is durable and can dissipate into smoke if 'killed,' reforming 24 hours later. If you die, the familiar vanishes permanently.
objects.title: Familiar

objects.text: You can manifest a translucent hand of force within 10 meters of yourself. The hand can lift up to 5 kg, manipulate small objects, and deliver weak strikes. It can't penetrate solid barriers. Lasts up to a minute per use, with a short cooldown.
objects.title: Spectral Hand

objects.text: At any time, you can name a person, place, or object you've personally encountered. You'll instinctively know the direction to it from your current position, but not the distance. Only one target can be tracked at a time.
objects.title: Compass Sense

objects.text: Once per day, you can reroll a single outcome in your favor — an enemy's attack misses, your shot connects, the guard happens to look away at the right moment. The effect is subtle; onlookers will just think you got lucky. Cannot save you from certain death, only tilt a near-thing.
objects.title: Fortuna's Hand

objects.text: You can conjure a small pool of clear, drinkable water — enough to fill a large bucket — once per hour. The water is mildly healing; drinking it speeds recovery from minor wounds and fatigue. Doesn't work on diseases, poison, or major trauma.
objects.title: Wellspring

objects.text: By touching a container of food or water, you can neutralize any poison, disease, or contaminant within it. The process takes a few seconds and leaves the material otherwise unchanged. Doesn't work on living creatures directly.
objects.title: Purify

objects.text: You can summon a small floating light that follows you around for up to an hour. The light is bright enough to read by, can be moved mentally within 10 meters, and changes color on command. Multiple wisps can be summoned; each adds to the aura cost.
objects.title: Will-o-wisp

objects.text: Any door, chest, or container you touch can be magically locked. The lock can only be opened by you, by someone you specifically allow, or by sufficient force destroying the container. The lock lasts until you dismiss it or are more than a kilometer away.
objects.title: Lockward

objects.text: If you've lost something — keys, a weapon, a scroll, a person — you can summon a small glowing mote that floats in the direction of the nearest instance of what you're looking for. The mote doesn't distinguish between similar items (your keys vs. anyone else's keys) and disappears after a minute.
objects.title: Finder

objects.text: You can send a short mental message — up to thirty words — to anyone you've met in person. They hear it as if you whispered in their ear and can whisper a reply that you hear the same way. Range is planetary; doesn't work through certain magical barriers.
objects.title: Whisper

objects.text: Your presence calms those around you. Active aggression within roughly 20 meters of you fades to apathy, fear dulls to unease, and anger cools to irritation. The effect is subtle and continuous; people rarely notice it's happening. Doesn't work on Grimm, who feed on the residue you're smoothing away.
objects.title: Soothe

objects.text: You can read any written text, regardless of language or cipher, as if it were your native tongue. The text doesn't change — you just understand it. Doesn't help with spoken language. Magical texts and runic inscriptions still require study to actually use; you just know what they say.
objects.title: Lexicon

objects.text: You can reshape non-living materials you're touching — wood, stone, metal, glass — as if they were soft clay, for as long as you maintain contact. The material retains its original properties; you're just changing its shape. Living matter and dust are unaffected.
objects.title: Sculpt

objects.text: You can freeze a small object — up to the size of a basketball — in time for up to a minute. While frozen, the object is indestructible and immovable. Useful for stopping a thrown grenade, freezing a falling ally in midair, or pinning a small item in place. Long cooldown after each use.
objects.title: Stasis

objects.text: By maintaining eye contact with someone for three seconds, you can force them into a deep sleep that lasts up to ten minutes or until disturbed. Strong-willed targets can resist, and anyone in immediate danger gets a bonus to the save. Doesn't work on Grimm.
objects.title: Slumber

objects.text: You can form a telepathic bond with a single non-Grimm animal, allowing you to share senses and issue simple commands at any distance. The animal becomes loyal to you and won't attack you even if provoked. If the animal dies, you can bond a new one after a week of mourning.
objects.title: Beastbond

rows.title: Magic
rows.titleText: Magic just does stuff. In effect, magic is an out of context power that is versatile and has no clear power source, with its limits being unclear. Here, you can gain access to limited aspects of magic, a thing most people could only dream of. While the magical abilities offered are mostly not ostensibly strong, the apparent lack of a cost makes them considerably more powerful. These are minor boons meant to complement your other choices. As such, they cannot become any stronger than what is listed, though there is potential for creative use. None of these abilities can be directly activated inside of the body of a living creature or Grimm unless otherwise specified.

objects.title: Relic Potential
objects.text: Unlocks your capacity to form an Affinity with any of the four Relics. Without this, their power will always be closed to you.

rows.title: Relics
rows.titleText: The four Relics of Beacon, Haven, Atlas, and Shade are not bought here — they're the domain of headmasters, kingdoms, and the occasional catastrophic theft. What you can buy is an Affinity: a thread of resonance with one Relic's spirit, grown well before you ever lay a hand on the real thing. Should you one day actually take hold of a Relic you carry a maxed Affinity for, ownership passes to you — not the gods, not the kingdoms that guard it, but you — and your Affinity with it grows stronger still.

objects.title: Affinity Tier 1
objects.text: A subconscious awareness of your surroundings out to a few dozen meters, warning you of danger and presence before your physical senses ever could. It also makes learning come easily: new knowledge sticks, and skills you practice settle in faster than they should.

objects.title: Affinity Tier 2
objects.text: Your sixth sense fully matures into something closer to an old god's understanding of knowledge itself. This isn't mere premonition — it's context beyond what your physical senses could ever supply. Walk into a room and know who stood there yesterday and who will stand there tomorrow. Look at a machine you've never seen and grasp its logic on sight. Watch someone's face and see the betrayal in them ten steps before they've even thought to make it.

objects.title: Affinity Tier 3
objects.text:
Beyond the vast growth to your sixth sense — now pulling context from miles away and weeks in every direction of time — you gain the ability to simply ask, as if Jinn herself were listening, and receive an answer once a month.

The knowledge comes complete with the skill to use it: ask about the science behind a hyperdrive from Star wars a few times and you could start building one; ask how a sword saint moves and your hand will carry a saint's unseen skill.

This to note, only grants knowledge and comprehension, it does not add the impossible to the possible — if what you want is a power like Chakra, you'll need to also ask how to make it yourself.

rows.title: Relic of Knowledge
rows.titleText: A golden lamp holding a spirit named Jinn, who can answer any question about the past or present. Left to herself, she grants three questions every hundred years — but an Affinity of your own lets you draw on a sliver of that same insight, on your own schedule.

objects.title: Affinity Tier 1
objects.text: An artisan's instinct settles into your hands and eyes: you can spot a structural flaw or a clumsy design at a glance, and your own crafting comes out cleaner and faster than it has any right to, as if you'd trained under a master for years. You can also conjure small, simple objects from a clear mental image alone — no schematic required — though they don't last long and won't hold up to much stress.

objects.title: Affinity Tier 2
objects.text: You become inhumanly efficient and precise at building anything you set your mind to, completing in days what would take a full team of master artisans, Atlas engineers, and blacksmiths a month. Every so often, given enough time and care, you can produce a Magnum Opus — a single work so perfect it bends physics in small, strange ways.

objects.title: Affinity Tier 3
objects.text: You gain a path to victory, but for creation: think of what you want to build, and your mind instantly extrapolates and simulates the best way to make it, handing you the knowledge and skill to do it even if you never learned them. From advance science, to arcane arts, whatever piece of knowledge you need, it all flows seamlessly into your hands. You also gain a workshop tucked into another dimension, built perfectly to your needs, with every tool you could ask for and comfortable enough to live in. Only materials is missing — even then, veins of ore run clear from the ground and a forest grows around your workshop, but they are entirely mundane; anything magical or advanced has to be grown or brought in yourself. The workshop can also speed up its own time by up to a hundredfold, so a hundred days inside can pass in a single day outside.

rows.title: Relic of Creation
rows.titleText: An ornate staff holding a spirit named Ambrosius, who can create anything so long as he's given highly specific schematics. He can only sustain one active creation at a time — conjuring something new unmakes whatever came before it.

objects.title: Affinity Tier 1
objects.text: Your whole physique improves noticeably — strength, toughness, and reflexes all rise together — paired with an instinctive knowledge of exactly how to break things: where something is weakest, how much force a strike needs, and how to make it count.

objects.title: Affinity Tier 2
objects.text:
You can cloak your body in a destructive aura that massively enhances your movements and strikes, though it only holds for a few moves or seconds before it burns out and needs to be raised again.

Forced through a weapon instead, that same aura erupts outward as a beam or a slash of pure destruction.

objects.title: Affinity Tier 3
objects.text: You've become an avatar of destruction wearing a human shape. Your punch now leaves deep craters, your kicks destroy buildings, and you can move faster than the eyes can see, and all of this is without your cloak. Not like you need them now, you're enough of a savant at destruction that a single, correctly placed strike could bring down an entire tower — and that sense for breaking things stops being purely physical, extending to concepts like relationships, careers, and the web of trust that holds a society together. When subtlety stops mattering however, your destructive Aura now becomes something you can wear comfortably and continuously, destruction woven over your body for hours with nary a sweat. Use tanker ship like clubs, walk atop bullets frozen in mid air, and sleep through the entire floating city of Atlas falling upon you.

rows.title: Relic of Destruction
rows.titleText: A sword of pure darkness capable of rending and destroying anything. Its only real limitation is itself: a weak hand wielding it is overwhelmed by its power, unleashing a shockwave of destruction for miles around, while a strong and steady hand can instead cut through space itself with total precision. It is kept in a secure vault hidden beneath Shade Academy in Vacuo.

objects.title: Affinity Tier 1
objects.text: You gain the ability to glimpse the future — though the farther ahead you try to look, the more possibilities blur and overlap into noise. Your mind also grows naturally sharper at spotting patterns and likely outcomes in everyday situations, even without consciously trying.

objects.title: Affinity Tier 2
objects.text: You can now see minutes ahead with real clarity, and calculate days ahead with striking accuracy — 80 to 90%+ out to a week, and a fair bit further with effort. Alongside this comes a precognitive warning sense, an instinctive flinch from danger before it arrives. Your mind starts to work like a dedicated supercomputer.

objects.title: Affinity Tier 3
objects.text:
This is where it stops being reasonable.

Your foresight now runs weeks, if not over a month, into the future, with a command of numbers, probabilities, and branching outcomes that makes any supercomputer look quaint — and it all runs quietly in the background, no conscious effort required. With this you can dodge attacks, predict future actions, identify weak points, sense the surrounding area through vibrations, perfectly measure things with a glance, leverage your strength perfectly, and much more.

You wouldn't have a hard time predicting and manipulating the world economy

rows.title: Relic of Choice
rows.titleText: A crown that grants its wearer random visions of future choices they'll face. Its precise location has become a major point of contention and intrigue in its own right.

objects.title: Melee - Blunt
objects.text: Your weapon can be used to smash things. This includes hammers, nunchucks, and shields.

objects.title: Melee - Piercing
objects.text: Your weapon can be used to pierce things. This includes daggers, spears, and other thrusting weapons.

objects.title: Melee - Slashing
objects.text: Your weapon can be used to slash things. This includes swords, axes, and scythes, if you're into that.

objects.title: Melee - Worn
objects.text: Your weapon can be worn on the body. This includes gauntlets and claws.

objects.title: Ranged - Short Distance
objects.text: Your weapon's gun functionality has an effective range of up to 50 meters. This is where most handguns, shotguns, and SMGs would be.

objects.title: Ranged - Medium Distance
objects.text: Your weapon's gun functionality has an effective range of up to 200 meters. This is where infantry rifles would be.

objects.title: Ranged - Long Distance
objects.text: Your weapon's gun functionality has an effective range of over 600 meters. This is where sniper rifles would be.

objects.title: Ranged - Other
objects.text: Your ranged weapon isn't a firearm at all. In the case of longbows, this can make its effective range considerably higher than if it were a regular gun, but presents other challenges.

objects.title: Dust Compatible
objects.text:
Your weapon is in some way able to channel dust, letting it use elemental effects and limited gravity manipulation.

Opens Dust section

objects.title: Ars Magus
objects.text: Dust is a part of your weapon's main functions, such as using hard light dust to create a floating shield, combustion and gravity dust to negate its weight and propel itself at extreme speeds, or fire dust to coat it in flame and shoot fire projectiles. This is as close as you can really get to a magical weapon.

objects.title: Self-Sustaining
objects.text: Your weapon's stores of dust refill while unobserved once weekly. Don't ask how it works, it is a unique quality of your weapon you can't reproduce.

objects.title: Mechashift
objects.text: Much like Ruby's Crescent Rose, your weapon can switch between its forms. Somehow, the number of moving parts required for this aren't prone to failure.

objects.title: Common Item
objects.text: Your weapon has a form that can pass as a mundane object, like Coco's minigun turning into a handbag.

objects.title: Dual Wielding
objects.text: Instead of one weapon, you actually have two, which can optionally combine into a bigger weapon. This is normally impractical, though the especially skilled, such as Maria Calavera, can make it work.

objects.title: Badass
objects.text: Your weapon looks really badass, regardless of your other choices in this section.

Your weapon is genuinely massive — think Zwei-sized hammer, two-handed greatsword, or something Nora would call 'a bit much.' Hits land like a truck, but the swing speed is slow and you'll telegraph your attacks. Best paired with a fighting style that embraces the wind-up rather than trying to hide it.
objects.title: Heavy

objects.text: Your weapon can be hidden on your person in a way that defeats casual pat-downs and most security scanners. Could be a collapsible design, a folding mechanism, or just unusually thin. This is different from Common Item — your weapon still looks like a weapon when deployed, it just hides well when not.
objects.title: Concealed

objects.text: Your weapon can extend its reach on demand — a whip-sword, a telescoping spear, a chain-weight, your call. Useful for keeping Grimm at the wrong distance and for surprising foes who thought they were outside your range. The extended form is less durable than the compact one, so don't try to block heavy hits with a fully-extended blade.
objects.title: Extending Reach

objects.text: Your weapon has a part that spins — a chain, a propeller, a flail head, a saw blade. The spinning component adds rotational force to your swings and can be used to deflect projectiles (with practice). Expect maintenance to be a chore; those moving parts don't lubricate themselves.
objects.title: Spinner

objects.text: Your weapon is built to take hits that would shatter a normal one. Use it to block heavy Grimm strikes, parry another huntsman's greatsword, or pry open an armored door. The reinforcement adds weight, so swings will be slightly slower — but the weapon will outlast you in any reasonable fight.
objects.title: Reinforced

objects.text: Your weapon has built-in propulsion — small dust jets or compressed-air thrusters — that can add serious force to a swing or stabilize a recoil-heavy shot. The boost is short-lived and needs to recharge between uses, but timed correctly, it can turn a regular hit into a knockout blow.
objects.title: Booster

objects.text: Your weapon has a limited AI assistant built in. It can recall itself to your hand magnetically, suggest optimal melee timing, auto-correct aim on ranged shots, and warn you when something's about to flank you. It's not sentient, just very well-programmed. Don't expect it to hold a conversation; do expect it to make you look better than you are.
objects.title: Smart Weapon

objects.text: Your weapon can swap components in the field — change the blade type, swap the barrel, attach a shield, remove the weight. The swaps take a few seconds each, which is forever in combat, but between fights you can reconfigure for any situation. Carry the parts in a backpack and you've got a small armory on your shoulders.
objects.title: Modular

objects.text: Your weapon is, in some limited sense, awake. It can sense danger and vibrate to warn you, react to your emotional state, and even move on its own in small ways — nudging your parry an inch to the left, tilting a guard to catch a shot you didn't see. It's not smart enough to fight for you, but it'll make you feel like you're cheating. Don't ask where it came from.
objects.title: Living Weapon

objects.text: Your weapon has a built-in grenade launcher — underslung, top-mounted, your choice. The grenades are dust-based and reasonably powerful, with a small magazine of 4-6 shots before reload. Excellent for area denial and breaking up clusters, less excellent for precision work.
objects.title: Grenadier

objects.text: Your ranged weapon has a small blade attachment for close-range stabs. This isn't a substitute for a real melee weapon, but it'll discourage anyone trying to grab your barrel and gives you a last-ditch option when they get inside your range. Pair with a sidearm for best results.
objects.title: Bayonet

objects.text: When thrown, your weapon returns to your hand after a few seconds — magnetically recalled, dust-propelled, or just aerodynamically designed, your call. Lets you make ranged throws with a melee weapon without losing it. The return path is predictable, so a clever enemy can intercept it; a not-clever enemy will get hit twice.
objects.title: Boomerang

objects.text: Your weapon's blade (or striking surface) is made of hard light dust projection. It can be turned on and off at will, change shape slightly to suit the situation, and is weightless when 'off.' Requires dust to maintain, but the dust lasts weeks per charge. Particularly nasty against Grimm, who can't grip what isn't really there.
objects.title: Hard Light Edge

objects.text: Your weapon incorporates a range of utility functions beyond combat: a built-in grapple launcher, lockpicks in the hilt, a small welding torch, a scroll-charging port, etc. None of these are as good as dedicated equipment, but having them all in one package saves a lot of backpack space.
objects.title: Multi-Tool

objects.text: Your weapon hits much harder than its size would suggest. Some trick of engineering (or dust, or something stranger) means a weapon that looks like a rapier hits like a greatsword. The weight is still there when you swing it — you just don't have to carry it when you're not. The catch: the stored mass adds to recoil on ranged shots, so your aim will suffer.
objects.title: Stored Mass

rows.title: Weapon
rows.titleText: A huntsman's weapon is often a unique, specialized one made to fit their exact fighting style. Due to the commonality of a transforming component or built-in gun function, almost every huntsman, even those who specialize in melee weapons, always has a gun on hand. This is where you design your weapon. You'll automatically have the design and engineering skills to make the weapon yourself, in addition to starting with it already built.

objects.title: Fire
objects.text: Generates fire in various forms.

objects.title: Ice
objects.text: Generates ice in various forms.

objects.title: Wind
objects.text: Generates air currents in various forms and levels of intensity.

objects.title: Lightning
objects.text: Generates electric charges and lightning.

objects.title: Water
objects.text: Generates water. What else would it do?

objects.title: Plant
objects.text: Not currently clear, but it probably makes plants grow.

objects.title: Rock
objects.text: Generates rock in the form of projectiles and walls.

objects.title: Gravity
objects.text: Generates a localized gravitational field for either attraction or repulsion.

objects.title: Hard Light
objects.text: Generates hard light projections such as barriers, platforms, and mimicries of other peoples' weapons.

objects.title: Combustion
objects.text: This seems to be capable of creating fiery projectiles when launched, melt ice, and reshape molten glass.

objects.title: Steam
objects.text: Generates clouds of steam. It's created by combining fire and water dust.

rows.title: Dust
rows.titleText: What types of dust is your weapon using? Dust is a substance mined out of the ground that can be used for various effects. It's basically magic fossil fuels. Note that none of them are particularly expensive for a huntsman, though there's a limit to how much you want to rely on dust instead of your own innate capabilities. If it seems like I don't know exactly how what does what, that's because RWBY itself isn't the clearest about things.

objects.title: Money
objects.text: As a freebie, you'll be finding the equivalent of $20,000 USD in your local currency after your insertion into the world, so you won't start with nothing. Nobody will question you getting this money.

objects.title: House
objects.text: As a freebie, you start with a house that is fully paid off, can reasonably accommodate five people, and has all modern appliances required for you to live comfortably. While you still need to pay bills, you have no mortgage to worry about.

objects.title: Combat Outfit
objects.text: This is an outfit of your design that is made with a special fabric, making it function as low level armor for your day-to-day life. Yes, it'll be fashionable and fit your main color.

objects.title: Scroll
objects.text: This is basically just a smartphone. It does all of the smartphone stuff as well as tracking the Aura reserves of yourself and your team and can be upgraded further.

objects.title: Student ID
objects.text: This is proof that you're a current student of a Huntsman Academy. If you haven't actually started yet, this can instead be an acceptance letter.

objects.title: Hunter License
objects.text: No, not like that. This is proof that you're a huntsman/huntress, making you one of a small group qualified to fight Grimm. There's a lot of situations where this will get you respect and smooth things over.

objects.title: Wardrobe
objects.text: You have 5 sets of non-combat tailored clothing made to suit your tastes.

objects.title: Lucky Charm
objects.text: Created through an unknown means, this charm grants an increase to luck in day-to-day life, though it doesn't seem effective during combat. This does not counter the Unlucky drawback.

objects.title: Cloak of the Unknown
objects.text: When wearing this robe, you'll be impossible to recognize for those directly looking at you.

objects.title: Motorcycle
objects.text: Now you have a top-of-the-line motorcycle of your choosing that somehow refuels overnight at no cost to yourself. You cannot reverse engineer or reproduce this, so don't try.

objects.title: Car
objects.text: Now you have a top-of-the-line car of your choosing that somehow refuels overnight at no cost to yourself. You cannot reverse engineer or reproduce this, so don't try.

objects.title: Armored Vehicle
objects.text: Your car is reinforced to the point that most firearms and some weaker Grimm would struggle to damage it at all.

objects.title: Blessed Ring
objects.text: This ring doubles the rate of healing for anyone wearing it, boosting their ability to heal from specifically wounds and diseases.

objects.title: Hunter's Ring
objects.text: This ring is a relic belonging to a man once considered a great hero. When wearing it, your ability to control your aura is boosted considerably. The boost makes it much easier to use Aura to shield yourself and do advanced techniques.

objects.title: Mage's Ring
objects.text: Once worn by a powerful queen, this ring increases the range of one's magical abilities by roughly 50%, if applicable.

objects.title: Morpher
objects.text: You have a worn device of some kind, such as a belt, that when activated by a set gesture or phrase, will use hard light dust to create strong armor of your design overtop your body. The dust required will refill overnight, so don't worry about costs.

objects.title: Property
objects.text: This lets you customize your House and the land it's on.

A palm-sized device that points toward the nearest Grimm, with a small readout for approximate size and number. Range is roughly a kilometer. Invaluable for anyone traveling outside the walls — most huntsmen would trade their secondary weapon for one of these.
objects.title: Grimm Compass

objects.text: A military-grade encrypted communicator that bypasses civilian scroll networks. Range is planetary (via relay towers) and the encryption is good enough that even Atlas R&D would need weeks to crack it. Comes pre-loaded with the contact info for one general, who may or may not take your calls.
objects.title: Atlas Communicator

objects.text: A small leather pouch, roughly the size of a fanny pack, that opens into a personal pocket dimension. Holds about as much as a small closet — enough for a few changes of clothes, a week's rations, and your weapon. Weight is reduced to roughly 10% inside the pouch. Don't put living things in it; they don't come back right.
objects.title: Pocket Dimension Pouch

objects.text: A small, well-made pouch with several internal compartments for different types of dust. Holds roughly 20 charges worth of dust total. The pouch itself is dust-resistant, so even combustion dust won't go off if jostled. A practical accessory for anyone using Dust Compatible or Ars Magus weapons.
objects.title: Dust Pouch

objects.text: A compact, well-stocked medical kit designed for huntsman use. Includes aura-friendly bandages, antitoxins, painkillers, a tourniquet, and a small supply of dust-based coagulants. Enough to stabilize one serious wound or treat several minor ones. Won't save you from a missing limb, but it'll buy you time to get to someone who can.
objects.title: First Aid Kit

objects.text: A palm-sized reconnaissance drone with a 2-kilometer range, 30-minute battery, and a camera that streams directly to your scroll. Silent, small, and surprisingly durable. Useful for scouting Grimm nests, peeking over walls, and confirming that yes, the bandit camp is exactly as fortified as you feared.
objects.title: Spy Drone

objects.text: A wrist-mounted grappling launcher with 30 meters of high-tensile cable. Strong enough to support your weight plus a passenger, with a motorized winch for fast ascents. Comes with three hook types: claw (for grabbing surfaces), magnetic (for metal), and piercing (for Grimm carapaces, if you're feeling aggressive).
objects.title: Grappling Hook

objects.text: A small earpiece that auto-translates any known language in real-time, including several dead ones and a few that probably shouldn't be on the list. Also works as a regular earpiece for your scroll. Won't help you speak the languages — only understand them — but that's usually the harder half.
objects.title: Translator Earpiece

objects.text: A high-quality wilderness survival kit: a lightweight tent that sleeps two, a sleeping bag rated for cold weather, a firestarter, a water purifier, and a small cookset. All packs down to the size of a large book. Designed for huntsmen who routinely spend weeks outside the walls.
objects.title: Camping Gear

objects.text: A professional-quality camera with a powerful zoom lens, night-vision mode, and a built-in scroll link for instant photo transfer. Useful for documenting Grimm, recording evidence, or just taking nice pictures of the countryside. The zoom is good enough to read a nametag from 200 meters.
objects.title: Camera

objects.text: A pair of stylish boots with reinforced soles and ankle support. Kicks hit significantly harder, falls from up to three stories don't break your legs, and you can walk on jagged rocks without complaint. They also don't slip on ice, which is more useful than you'd think.
objects.title: Reinforced Boots

objects.text: A dummy scroll that looks and feels like a real one, but runs a honeypot OS. If anyone tries to hack it, it'll feed them fake data and trigger a small incendiary charge, destroying the device and lightly burning the would-be thief's hands. The explosion is non-lethal but very loud.
objects.title: Decoy Scroll

objects.text: A family heirloom of your choosing — a sword, a pendant, a ring, a worn journal — that has accumulated just a touch of sentimental power over the generations. The exact effect is small and specific to the item: a sword that's slightly luckier in parries, a pendant that warns of poison, a journal that sometimes has helpful marginalia appear in it. Decide what it is and what it does.
objects.title: Heirloom

objects.text: A silver charm bracelet that can hold up to three minor magical charms. Comes with one charm of your choosing (small luck boost, minor healing, or limited danger sense). Additional charms can be found, bought, or earned through missions. Charms can be swapped in and out, but only one of each type can be active at a time.
objects.title: Charm Bracelet

objects.text: An ornate pocket watch with a single, very specific power: once per day, when you click it, your perception of time slows to roughly a third of normal for ten seconds. From your perspective, the world crawls; from everyone else's, you just look unusually sharp. Doesn't actually speed you up — you can't move faster — but you can think, plan, and aim in that window.
objects.title: Pocket Watch

objects.text: Any clothing you own will, when not being worn, slowly clean and repair itself. Stains lift, tears mend, odors fade — all over the course of a few hours. The effect only works on clothes you actually own (no cleaning strangers' laundry for them), and it doesn't add new features, just maintains what's there.
objects.title: Self-Cleaning Clothes

objects.text: A military ID pendant from the Atlas Military, identifying you as a contracted specialist. Grants access to Atlas military facilities, discounts at SDC-affiliated stores, and the right to legally carry weapons in Atlas-controlled territory. Atlas is gone post-fall, but the badge still opens doors in what's left of it.
objects.title: Atlas Pendant
Large House
objects.text: Your House can now reasonably accommodate ten or so people. Everything is still already paid for, with appliances being fairly high-end.

addons.title: Servant Quarters
addons.text: If you'd like, the servants will have their own designated quarters, separate from the spaces meant for you, your family, and your guests.

objects.title: Manor
objects.text: Your House, if it can be called that anymore, can now accommodate upwards of 20 people. Everything is still paid for, with appliances being top of the line and you owning one acre of land around your House.

objects.title: Laboratory
objects.text: You have a state-of-the-art lab that is large enough to contain and experiment on roughly human-sized Grimm, such as Beowolves.

objects.title: Servants
objects.text: You have (fully paid for) staff to help clean and care for your home, with the number scaling with your House. At base you'll have one, a Large House allows up to two, and a Manor allows up to five. You can choose their general appearances and personalities, but they will have no meaningful abilities beyond the general duties expected of a maid or butler. By default, they live in the House with you.

objects.title: Five Acres
objects.text: You have five acres of usable land to your name, which is enough to comfortably make a family of four meaningfully self-sufficient. Your land is exceptionally good for farming.

objects.title: Hundred Acres
objects.text: You have one hundred acres of land to your name, which is enough for the grazing required to raise cattle. You'll be able to make a fairly good living as a farmer, as long as you can keep Grimm away from your property.

objects.title: Thousand Acres
objects.text: You have one thousand acres of land to your name, enough to run a midsize commercial farm. If you become a farmer, you could go your entire life without financial struggle.

objects.title: Dust Veins
objects.text: Your property has a natural cave system that, when explored, will reveal a large amount of dust waiting to be mined. This will require that you own land, otherwise the cave will be under your own home.

objects.title: Refilling Veins
objects.text: Over the course roughly a decade, the dust in your cave system will slowly reappear, letting you mine it again. People will question how exactly this works.

objects.title: Defenses
objects.text: Your House and property, whether together or separate, have a security team capable of dealing with anything short of a team of huntsmen or a group of 5-10 Grimm. While fully paid for, they are not guaranteed to be capable of teaching you to fight.

objects.title: Armory
objects.text: You have a fairly sizable armory. While it doesn't have any mechashift weapons, you do have enough guns, melee weapons, and ammo to supply a few dozen people. The weapons are fairly standard, being what you might expect of an Atlesian solider.

objects.title: Restocking
objects.text: The kitchen seems to be vaguely magical, with mundane ingredients, like flour, sugar, and fresh produce, simply appearing where they're meant to once a week. Food that appears this way will turn to dust after 7 days of going unused.

objects.title: Personal Dimension
objects.text: You can use this to have your choices in this section apply to the Home island instead of an area in Remnant. By default, you'll still have the basic House in your starting location, so don't worry about not having a proper home address.

rows.title: Property
rows.titleText: This is where you customize your House, the land it's on, and any other land you might own. If you live on more than an acre of land, your home will be necessarily located outside of the major cities.

objects.title: Functional Adult
objects.text: Being a productive member of society is important, though hard for many. This does not apply to you, someone who is capable of working most mundane jobs that don't require specialized knowledge or post-secondary education.

objects.title: Serious Business
objects.text: You always know how to carry yourself in a way that makes you look like a big deal. Even in fairly stressful situations, you can appear confident and even a bit cool. One can only hope that you're as confident as you look.

objects.title: Techie
objects.text: When it comes to most technology, you know how to disassemble and reassemble it, in addition to knowing how to make a mechashift weapon. If you aren't a huntsman, you'll be a great support for one.

objects.title: Teaching
objects.text: Some people are bad at teaching others, but you excel at it. You're skilled at breaking down subject matter of any kind, as long as you understand it, in ways personalized to ensure nearly any student understands it.

objects.title: Stealth
objects.text: You have a knack for going unseen. You can easily measure a distance, predict movements, and remain observant of a target's line of sight to remain undetected.

objects.title: Linguist
objects.text: You're fluent in one additional language and also generally good at learning languages too. Given that all of Remnant speaks one language, this is likely the equivalent of speaking fluent Latin in the 21st century.

objects.title: Ambidextrous
objects.text: You can use both your left and right hands so proficiently that people won't know which was originally your dominant hand.

objects.title: Improvement Sense
objects.text: You will always know whether or not your practice in any given skill is effective, though you won't instinctively know better methods.

objects.title: Combat Trained
objects.text: You know how to handle yourself in a fight. You're not the most experienced with any fighting style or weapon, but you can do better than most in the majority of situations. This is a good foundation for any further training.

objects.title: Weapons Training
objects.text: You've been trained for 2-4 years in whatever melee weapon forms you chose in the Weapon section. This will not make you an elite fighter, but you'll be much better than most.

objects.title: Weapon Mastery
objects.text: You're an expert in whatever melee weapon forms you chose in the weapon section. Most people would need a decade of training to be comparable to your skills.

objects.title: Active Shooter
objects.text: You've been trained in marksmanship for 2-4 years, meaning you'll be a pretty good shot with any ranged weapon forms you chose in the Weapon section.

objects.title: Deadeye
objects.text: You're an expert marksman, meaning that you're a nearly perfect shot with and ranged weapon forms chosen in the Weapon section.

objects.title: Unarmed Training
objects.text: You've got 2-4 years of training as a martial artist, making you much better in hand to hand than the majority of people ever will be.

objects.title: Black Belt
objects.text: With your level of martial expertise, it'd take upwards of a decade of dedicated practice for someone to be comparable to your skills.

objects.title: Strategist
objects.text: With how good you are at making and executing plans, you're able to outthink most people. Your plans are not guaranteed to succeed but are close to perfect for the amount of intel you have in any given situation.

objects.title: Leader
objects.text: Unlike Ruby, you could turn a group of unprepared teens into a group of heroes within weeks and ensure that they can work together regardless of any interpersonal problems.

objects.title: Medical
objects.text: You're no doctor, but you certainly know how to identify and treat common ailments, as well as a solid grasp on human biology, which serves as a good foundation for actually going to medical school.

You're a skilled driver of any ground vehicle — cars, motorcycles, trucks, even the occasional tank if you find yourself in one. You can pull off precision maneuvers at speed, recover from slides, and squeeze through gaps that would make a driving instructor weep. Just don't expect this to translate to aircraft.
objects.title: Driver

objects.text: You can fly — anything from a Bullhead to a small airship to an Atlas gunship. Takeoffs, landings, evasive maneuvers, even basic dogfighting if it comes up. Airships are expensive, so this skill is most useful if you have a way to borrow or steal one. Atlas academy includes this in the curriculum; everyone else has to learn the hard way.
objects.title: Pilot

objects.text: You can repair and maintain most mechanical systems — engines, mechashift mechanisms, scroll internals, vehicle drivetrains. This won't let you build anything new (see Techie for that), but you can keep existing equipment running with duct tape and determination. A vital skill for any huntsman on a budget.
objects.title: Mechanic

objects.text: You can actually cook. Not just 'follow a recipe,' but genuinely turn random ingredients into enjoyable meals. You'll eat well on the trail, impress dates, and save a small fortune on restaurants. Teammates will fight over who gets to share your kitchen. Note: this does not include the ability to source ingredients; that's a separate problem.
objects.title: Cook

objects.text: You're a skilled musician on at least one instrument of your choice. Performance-level skill, able to play professionally if you wanted to. Useful for cover identities, distracting guards, and the occasional undercover gig at a shady nightclub. Also just nice to have.
objects.title: Musician

objects.text: You can act. Not 'community theater' act — convincingly adopt a persona, maintain an accent for hours, lie to someone's face without a tell. Useful for undercover work, talking your way past checkpoints, and convincingly pretending to be a foreign exchange student from a kingdom nobody's heard of.
objects.title: Actor

objects.text: You're skilled at formal negotiation — hostage situations, contract disputes, ransom demands, tense standoffs between factions. You know how to read a counterparty, make concessions that don't actually cost anything, and walk away from a bad deal. This is a more specialized skill than general charisma.
objects.title: Negotiator

objects.text: You're skilled at getting information from unwilling sources. You know when to play good cop, when to play bad cop, when to just sit quietly and let the silence do the work. You're also good at spotting when someone is feeding you a line. Whether you use this ethically is up to you.
objects.title: Interrogator

objects.text: You can follow tracks through most terrain — footprints in mud, broken twigs, disturbed dust. You can identify what made them (human, faunus, Grimm, animal) and roughly how long ago. Works best in wilderness; cities are harder but not impossible.
objects.title: Tracker

objects.text: You can survive in the wilderness indefinitely. Build a shelter, find water, start a fire, navigate by stars. You won't be comfortable, but you won't die. Most huntsmen have a crash course in this; you have the full version.
objects.title: Survivalist

objects.text: You can forge and repair weapons, armor, and tools from raw materials. Not mechashift mechanisms (that's Techie territory) but solid blades, sturdy armor, and functional hardware. A rare skill in modern Remnant, where most weapons come from specialists. Yours will be unique.
objects.title: Blacksmith

objects.text: You can refine raw dust into more potent forms — combining types, purifying low-grade ore, even creating custom blends. Refined dust hits harder and lasts longer than the raw stuff. The SDC has whole facilities for this; you can do it on a workbench. Just mind the explosions.
objects.title: Dust Refiner

objects.text: You're trained in emergency medical response — triage, field surgery, stabilization under fire. This builds on Medical; where Medical is 'identify and treat common ailments,' First Responder is 'keep someone alive long enough to get to a hospital.' You'll be the most popular person on any team after a bad fight.
objects.title: First Responder

objects.text: You can pickpocket, palm objects, and perform sleight of hand with practiced ease. Coins, scrolls, small weapons — if it fits in your hand, you can move it without being noticed. Mostly useful for thieves and stage magicians, but occasionally handy for planting evidence or lifting a keycard.
objects.title: Sleight of Hand

objects.text: You can pick mechanical locks and bypass electronic ones. From cheap padlocks to high-end digital keypads, you've got a tool and a technique for each. Won't help with biometric scanners or magically-sealed doors, but those are rarer than you'd think.
objects.title: Lockpicker

objects.text: You're a trained acrobat — flips, wall jumps, tightrope walking, the works. In combat, this translates to fluid movement through complex environments and the ability to dodge in ways most fighters can't. Aura helps, but the underlying skill is yours. Looks great on camera.
objects.title: Acrobat

objects.text: You have academic-level knowledge of Grimm — species, behaviors, weaknesses, migration patterns, the leading theories on their origin. You can identify a Grimm from a silhouette at 500 meters and know which ones are pushovers versus which ones will eat your team. Useful for any huntsman, vital for any team leader.
objects.title: Grimm Scholar

objects.text: You have a thorough grounding in the history of Remnant — the Great War, the founding of the kingdoms, the Faunus Rights Revolution, the various smaller conflicts. You know which grudges are still active, which alliances are recent, and which old treaties might still be on the books somewhere. Surprisingly useful for navigating politics.
objects.title: Remnant Historian

rows.title: Skills
rows.titleText: Here you can spend points to guarantee being good at things beyond your current abilities.

objects.title: Rowan Blackmore
objects.text:
As the adopted son of two huntsmen, he's been instructed by his fathers since early childhood. In fact, Rowan is so used to being armed that he "feels naked" when he doesn't have a weapon on hand. Due to his natural talent and high work ethic, he tends to think of himself as better than his peers, which his perfectionist tendencies help him prove. He is considered one of Vale's most talented.

Out of a desire to always be armed, his weapons, collectively named Conquest, are a pair of boots that each have a built-in pistol. Additionally, he's learned to use his chain as a whip when need be. His semblance, Right to Rule, lets him see the way to achieve his own ideal victory, insofar as it doesn't require anything he finds particularly objectionable.

objects.title: Soleil Orléans
objects.text:
Born into one of the wealthiest families in Vale, Soleil's goal is to make herself and those loyal to her immortal. While some have questioned her motives, she claims to just want to make herself and the people loyal to her, who she does seem to care about, live happily. Her aura capacity and efficiency are notably above average.

Her weapon, Thorn, is a spear that has swappable cartridges that let her switch between fire, ice, wind, and lightning dust for a wide range of effects. Her semblance, Eternal Servitude, lets her sense those who have sworn loyalty to her and teleport them to her location. Her range is 1km.

objects.title: Tsune Akagi
objects.text:
Tsune, much like most Mistralian faunus, is not a stranger to discrimination or how difficult it is to deal with. Though many first think of him as just a pretty face, he's a loyal friend, proficient with dust usage, as well as a talented fighter and dancer. His goal is to use his high pay as a huntsman to open up a boutique.

His weapon is Reverie, his fan, which he imbued with fire and wind dust, letting him weave fire into dance, complemented by a high level of proficiency with martial arts. His semblance, Tenko, lets him summon a white fox, which can create illusions and grow to match his size. Tsune likes to let it hang around sometimes, which is why it's slumped over his shoulder.

objects.title: Penelope Argyros
objects.text:
Unlike most kids that grew up outside of major cities, Penelope had a fairly happy childhood, partially because of the huntsmen who moved to her small town. She heard their stories of fighting Grimm and now wants to follow in their footsteps, believing it to be the best way to get an exciting, fulfilling life.

Penelope's weapon, Copper Orchid, is a staff that can also turn into an assault rifle. Her semblance, Second Impact, lets her delay an attack's effects for up to a minute or until she lets it happen, letting her stack multiple hits to make one larger attack, depleting someone's aura and/or sending them flying.

objects.title: Charles Rothman
objects.text:
As the third child of a wealthy CEO, he knew it would be easier to get training to be a huntsman than to inherit the company, so he took the "easier path". Despite being generally aloof and seemingly well put together, he knows his limits and will ask for help when he needs it. He's unusually good with makeup because of how often his older sister had him help her with it instead of a servant.

His weapon, Kingmaker, is a luxurious briefcase that unfolds into 20 different forms and uses ammo imbued with several kinds of dust, letting him have a way to handle any enemy. His semblance, Appraisal, lets him instantly identify the physical and mental weaknesses of others, letting him easily exploit them.

objects.title: Karla Schwartz
objects.text:
Growing up in Mantle, beneath the floating city, Atlas, has taught Karla many things. The most important thing was that she would only be free when there was nobody above her. She resents her semblance because of how it confines her but has learned to use it to great effect. Her fighting style is known to be sadistic, with a tendency to taunt people when she thinks she's winning.

Like many people from Atlas, her weapon, Guiding Light, heavily uses dust. The weapon itself is just a golden handle and hilt that creates a blade made of hard light dust, but she's also working on a sidearm that uses other types of dust. Her semblance, Gravity, lets her change the gravity inside of closed rooms, whether increasing/decreasing it or outright changing what direction is down.

objects.title: Azraq Sharif
objects.text:
Known as one of the most talented huntsmen of his generation, Azraq's affinity for aura techniques has led to his fighting style revolving around them. Once a poor orphan on the streets of Vacuo, he was taken in by a retired huntsman who noticed his talent and took him in to raise and train.

He inherited his mentor's sword, Legacy, which he imbues with aura to fire projectile slash attacks while moving in fast, hard to predict ways. His semblance, Full Suit, lets him conjure up to 52 playing cards within a short range from his body and make them hover in place, which lets him deflect attacks and create footholds. He prefers to use Aura techniques instead of his semblance, so his mastery of it is questionable.

objects.title: Layla Karim
objects.text:
After the unfortunate deaths of her parents, she cared for her younger brother, Mahad, entirely on her own. She tends to be overly protective, even showing concern for her cat summon when it's harmed. Because of her rough childhood in Vacuo, she doesn't often extend her compassion to strangers.

Her weapon, Evolution, is a whip she made out of scrap fabric as a child but has continually upgraded over the years, including adding wind dust. Her semblance, Beckoning Cat, lets her summon a large cat that noticeably boosts her luck while it's active.

objects.title: Partner
objects.text: This is the choice for if you want your teammate to be the partner you made with the Partner perk. This is only actually important for the purpose of determining your team as a student because professional huntsmen aren't bound by the tradition of 4 member teams.

objects.title: Extra
objects.text: You know that a team is only meant to have 4 members, right? There's a whole fairy tale about it and everything. For every purchase of this, you can break that rule and get an extra team member.

rows.title: Team
rows.titleText: Every huntsman team has 4 members, yours being no exception. Here, you can choose the 3 people you either were/are/will be on a team with, depending on your age and whether you're at a huntsman academy. Their backstories will adjust based on chosen era and world mods if needed, and their age is close enough to your own that you all could be in the same graduating class.

objects.title: Saving the School
objects.text:
"Then the school blows up" is a line sometimes used to describe the fall of Beacon, an event which only happened because nobody involved knew enough to stop it. You, on the other hand, are fully aware of the fact that someone is planning something and intends on using the Vytal Festival as a way to sneak in and sabotage it to create enough chaos to secure a second objective at Beacon Academy. If you don't have metaknowledge for whatever reason, you'll know this much and nothing more. If there are no groups currently planning to destroy Beacon, then one will form, with Salem being partially responsible.

If you succeed in stopping this, then you'll set into motion a dismantling of whatever ongoing plans Salem has, ensuring that she won't be able to make any major moves for a solid twenty years at least.

objects.title: Proof of the Impossible
objects.text:
Magic, a power that exists beyond the bounds of dust and semblances with no clear cost, is something most people would believe doesn't exist. This understanding only persists because of Ozma's deliberate efforts to keep it a secret, though it's not if he has a reason other than covering up his own reincarnation process. You, however, can change things, revealing magic to the world, especially if you have magic of your own. After you start on this mission, you'll have exactly one year.

If you prove that magic exists to the world, in such a way that Ozma and his agents can't cover it up, then roughly 1% of the world's population will awaken to magic, each one roughly comparable to the three-point options, in addition to Hereditary. Even if you fail to prove that magic exists, Ozma and Salem will both somehow become fully aware of the magical and nonmagical abilities of yourself and your allies. The consequences of this will vary wildly depending on other choices and how you intend on living your life.

objects.title: Talent Poaching
objects.text:
Ozma and Salem have gotten quite good at ensuring that those with positions of power and/or noteworthy potential wind up following them, with Ozma wanting to preserve the world and Salem wanting to destroy it. You are a third party whose existence can tip the scales somewhat. With your presumed metaknowledge and powers, you can tip the scales by ensuring that someone completely switches sides or completely abandons the conflict between these feuding immortals. This guarantees that you'll meet a member of the faction of the one you're least aligned with, though you can choose which member, such as Cinder for Salem's faction, or Qrow for Ozma's.

If you successfully convince someone to leave Salem and join Ozma, the defector will give him valuable information that reveals Salem's current operatives, their capabilities, and her plans for them. If you convince someone to do the opposite, you'll guarantee a major victory for Salem, and a loss for civilization as a whole. If you fail, you will wind up becoming a known entity and possible threat, if you haven't already.

objects.title: Cross Tag Battle!
objects.text:
Not only is Team RWBY going to be sent into a space between worlds, where they're made to fight people from BlazBlue, Persona 4, and Under Night In-Birth for the chance to return home, but you'll be going with them! If you have a team, they'll be coming with you. There will be four keystones, which when collected, will allow the group who has them and everyone else from their world to return home.

If you/your team or team RWBY collect the keystones, then you'll all go home safely with no clue what happens to the others. If you/your team and team RWBY work with the people from the other worlds to dismantle the space you all find yourselves in, you'll all get to go home. If neither of these happens, then it's entirely up to chance what happens, with the worst-case scenario being that you're trapped in a space between worlds while yours is at some vague risk.

If you work with the people from other worlds to dismantle the space you were all sent to, then you'll personally get the ability to, once a week, open up a portal to one of the other worlds. The portal will last for roughly an hour and will appear at the location of a person in that other world that you know and are thinking of.

objects.title: Reviving/Saving Mountain Glenn
objects.text:
Mountain Glenn was meant to be the second city in the Kingdom of Vale, serving as a way to expand outward, reducing housing prices and proving that civilization expand beyond its four walled cities. For reasons most couldn't foresee (Salem), it was overrun by Grimm and left abandoned, becoming known as one of the greatest tragedies in modern history. It's your job to, depending on when you're starting, either prevent this or resettle Mountain Glenn.

If you succeed, then you'll be responsible for Remnant's fifth true city being able to thrive. Fate itself will ensure that Mountain Glenn stands for at least a century. If you fail, then Mountain Glenn's fate will remain the same, but you'll get to know that it's your fault first and foremost.

rows.title: Missions
rows.titleText: These are challenges you can willingly subject yourself to, with success having a reward and failure giving a proportionate punishment. These will only come up if you select the mission. While you can take any number of missions, it's best to know the limits of what you can handle.

defaultRowText: This is a row, and inside of it, you can place choices. On both rows and choices Requirements can be placed, which will block a row from being viewed, or make the player unable to select a choice, depending on either Point-types or the Ids of other choices. Point-types can be made in Features then Manage Points. Hovering over buttons will explain what they do. The Design of the project can be changed in 'Modify Design' at the side navigation bar, and private styling for each row can be turned on in the rows Settings. Default text like this can be turned off in Features -> Manage Defaults.
defaultChoiceText: This is a Choice, and inside of it, you can place images and text. Scores can be added to it, and have Point-types attached. Addons can be added underneath the image and text. In the Functions at the bottom of the choice, there is an array of different things that can be done. Default text like this can be turned off in Features then Manage Defaults.
